solution: With \( x=[[a]]\sin\theta \), \( dx=[[a]]\cos\theta\,d\theta \), the limits become \( \theta=0,\pi/2 \), and \( \sqrt{[[a]]^{2}-x^{2}}=[[a]]\cos\theta \). The integral becomes \( [[c]][[a]]^{2}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\cos^{2}\theta\,d\theta = [[c]][[a]]^{2}\cdot\dfrac{\pi}{4} = [[answer]] \); geometrically this is a quarter-circle of radius \( [[a]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( \pi/4 \)'s numerator-only form (half the circle's quarter area) instead of the full quarter-circle result.
This treats the region as a full square of side \( [[a]] \) rather than the quarter-disk the integral represents.
This uses a half-circle's area formula instead of a quarter-circle.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Using the substitution \( x = [[a]]\tan\theta \) (so \( x^{2}+[[a]]^{2} = [[a]]^{2}\sec^{2}\theta \)), evaluate \( \displaystyle\int_{0}^{[[a]]} \frac{[[c]]}{x^{2}+[[a]]^{2}}\,dx \).
solution: With \( x=[[a]]\tan\theta \), \( dx=[[a]]\sec^{2}\theta\,d\theta \), and \( x^{2}+[[a]]^{2}=[[a]]^{2}\sec^{2}\theta \), so the \( \sec^{2}\theta \) factors cancel: the integral becomes \( \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[a]]}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\pi/4} d\theta = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[a]]}\cdot\dfrac{\pi}{4} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the limit \( \theta=\pi/2 \) (as if \( x\to\infty \)) instead of the correct upper limit \( \theta=\pi/4 \) at \( x=[[a]] \).
This uses \( \theta=\pi \) as the upper limit, four times too large.
This drops the factor of \( \pi/4 \) entirely, as if the antiderivative were just \( \theta \) evaluated in \( x \)-units.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: With \( x=[[a]]\sec\theta \), \( dx=[[a]]\sec\theta\tan\theta\,d\theta \), and \( \sqrt{x^{2}-[[a]]^{2}}=[[a]]\tan\theta \), the limits become \( \theta=0 \) (at \( x=[[a]] \)) to \( \theta=\pi/3 \) (at \( x=2[[a]] \)). The integral becomes \( [[c]][[a]]^{2}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\pi/3}\sec\theta\tan^{2}\theta\,d\theta \), which evaluates to \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This keeps only the leading term \( \tfrac12 a^2\sqrt3 \) from evaluating \( \sec\theta\tan\theta \) at \( \theta=\pi/3 \) alone, dropping the logarithmic term from the reduction formula.
This mistakes the integrand for a constant-\( \sec\theta \) area and uses a \( \pi/3 \) sector formula instead of the actual antiderivative.
This treats \( \sqrt{x^2-a^2} \) as if it were bounded by \( a \) itself over the whole interval, giving a rectangle estimate.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: With \( x=[[a]]\sin\theta \), \( dx=[[a]]\cos\theta\,d\theta \), and \( \sqrt{[[a]]^{2}-x^{2}}=[[a]]\cos\theta \), the \( \cos\theta \) factors cancel and the integral becomes \( [[c]][[a]]^{2}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\sin^{2}\theta\,d\theta = [[c]][[a]]^{2}\cdot\dfrac{\pi}{4} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This doubles the correct \( \pi/4 \) constant to \( \pi/2 \).
This drops the \( \pi \) factor entirely, as if the average value of \( \sin^{2}\theta \) were rational.
This halves the correct \( \pi/4 \) constant.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: With \( x=[[a]]\tan\theta \), \( dx=[[a]]\sec^{2}\theta\,d\theta \), and \( (x^{2}+[[a]]^{2})^{3/2}=[[a]]^{3}\sec^{3}\theta \), the integral becomes \( [[c]]\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\pi/4}\sin^{2}\theta\,d\theta = [[c]]\left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}-\dfrac14\right) \)... after simplifying \( \tan^{2}\theta/\sec\theta = \sin^{2}\theta/\cos\theta \), the closed form works out to \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the antiderivative \( \ln(\sec\theta+\tan\theta) \) alone (appropriate for \( \sec\theta \), not \( \sin^{2}\theta/\cos\theta \)) evaluated at the limits.
This uses \( 1-\cos(\pi/4) \) as if the integrand reduced to \( \sin\theta \) rather than \( \sin^{2}\theta/\cos\theta \).
This reports only \( \sin(\pi/4) \) itself, mistaking the substituted variable's value for the integral's value.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
